answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation* (Vols. I–IV) does not offer a systematic or standalone definition of "liberty" comparable to Charles Dunoyer's explicit account in *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. I, Ch. 1 ("What the author means by the word liberty"), where liberty is "this *power* that man acquires to use his forces more easily *as he frees himself* from the obstacles that originally hindered their exercise"—a relative capacity growing with moral, industrial, and civilizational development across society.[^1][^2]**

Dunoyer ties liberty centrally to individual and societal progress: humans become freer by developing faculties (overcoming ignorance, vice, violence), regulating their use (self-restraint, justice), and advancing through stages from savagery to industrial society, where liberty peaks as "the true measure of liberty is civilization."[^3] He rejects "state of nature" myths of greater primitive liberty, insisting society is natural and liberty expands via enlightenment and morality.[^4]

Comte, by contrast, treats liberty incidentally within broader analysis of legislation and morality as sciences of facts—describing causes/effects of habits, laws, and institutions via the analytical method. Liberty appears as historical/legal outcomes of progress (e.g., "freedom of defense," abolition of torture, "freedom to express and publish one’s opinions"), emerging from analytical exposure of vicious laws/habits, not as a foundational "power" of faculty development.[^5][^6][^7] The sharpest implicit divergence arises in Vol. I, Ch. 11 ("CHAPITRE PREMIER"), where Comte defines *laws* themselves as inherent "powers" or forces composing a people's mode of existence/reproduction (ideas, needs, opinions, physical circumstances, mutual influences)—uniform in barbarism, diversifying with civilization.[^8] Liberty is not a distinct human power but embedded in these societal "laws" (broadly: natural forces regulating conduct), which legislators merely describe, not create.[^9]

Dunoyer centers liberty as *acquired personal/societal capacity* via moral-industrial self-overcoming; Comte subordinates it to *descriptive science of societal powers/laws*, where "freedom" manifests as institutional reforms from analysis (e.g., ending secrecy of procedures, imaginary crimes), without privileging faculty-freedom as liberty's essence.[^10] No single passage polemically contrasts Dunoyer, but Ch. 11's redefinition of law/power as pre-legislative societal forces most sharply reframes liberty away from Dunoyer's developmental individualism toward organic collective dynamics.

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Ch. 1 [p-fc8999](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).

[^2]: *Ibid.*, Ch. 1 [p-9b6829](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-9b6829) (echoed in related *Propriété* context, but per query).

[^3]: *Ibid.*, Ch. 1 [p-e80d8f](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-e80d8f).

[^4]: *Ibid.*, Ch. 1 [p-341c27](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-341c27).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Ch. 2 [p-614bf4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/#p-614bf4).

[^6]: *Ibid.*, Ch. 11 [p-177df7](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/11-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-177df7).

[^7]: *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, Ch. 15 [p-74a49f](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/15-de-l-influence-de-l-esclavage-domestique-sur-l-esp/translation/#p-74a49f) (liberty/freedom in slavery contexts).

[^8]: *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Ch. 11 [p-da4eb5](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/11-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-da4eb5); full chapter [citation_url](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/11-chapitre-premier/translation/).

[^9]: *Ibid.*, Ch. 11 [p-0bf2c4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/11-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-0bf2c4).

[^10]: Chs. 2–3 emphasize method's role in moral/legal progress yielding freedoms; no faculty-power focus.[^5]
